segments.title: Live question system setup
segments.summary: The stream explains how audience questions should be submitted so they can be tracked rather than lost in chat.
segments.body: This chapter is useful archive structure evidence: live-stream pages need Q&A segments that can preserve the prompt, timestamp, and source context separately from ordinary topical notes.

evidence.note: The transcript discusses adding questions to the question system.

segments.title: Modern Navy equivalent of the Blackburn Blackburn
segments.question: What is the modern Navy equivalent of the Blackburn Blackburn for this channel?
segments.answerShort: The prototype keeps this as a timestamped Q&A seed for later transcript-backed answer cleanup.
segments.summary: A live Q&A prompt asks for a modern Navy analogue to the Blackburn Blackburn, giving the prototype a real `qa` segment to render and search.
segments.body: This segment deliberately records the question and source window without over-polishing the answer. It proves that Q&A can remain a segment subtype while still carrying question-specific fields.

evidence.note: The transcript contains the modern Navy equivalent question and setup.

segments.title: Spanish 1950s destroyer escorts
segments.summary: A discussion of Spanish Navy destroyer escorts frames them as useful ships, but as gun-and-torpedo designs in a naval world moving beyond that model.
segments.body: The segment shows how ordinary subject notes should work: it is not a fabricated Q&A entry, but a topical point with timestamped transcript evidence and tags for search and browsing.

evidence.note: The transcript discusses Spanish Navy destroyer escorts and gun-and-torpedo destroyer design.

segments.title: Carrier group force structure sketch
segments.summary: A force-structure answer sketches a carrier group built around carriers, LHDs, air-defense destroyers, ASW frigates, and submarines.
segments.body: This segment tests multi-topic browsing and search metadata: the same timestamp belongs under carrier groups, naval aviation, destroyers, and modern navy planning.

evidence.note: The transcript lists carriers, LHDs, air-defense destroyers, ASW frigates, and SSNs around a carrier group.

segments.title: Ideal destroyers depend on era, but screening stays constant
segments.summary: The opening frames the ideal destroyer as era-dependent while keeping screening and task-group protection as the through-line.
segments.body: This is a useful organizing segment for destroyer coverage: the transcript separates offensive firepower from the more persistent role of protecting fleets, task groups, and ships from large and small threats.

evidence.note: Opening thesis explains that ideal destroyers change by decade but remain centered on screening and protection.

segments.title: Durand de la Penne as a destroyer and Oto Melara 76 mm story
segments.summary: The class is introduced as both an Italian Cold War destroyer subject and a way to follow the Oto Melara 76 mm from ship-design perspective.
segments.body: The source window links the class to Italian destroyer development and then moves into its missile and gun fit. It is a good first-pass anchor for later deeper coverage of the 76 mm, Otomat, and ship layout choices.

evidence.note: Transcript introduces the class as an Oto Melara 76 mm story and outlines the ship weapons fit.

segments.title: Near-term spending matters more than distant Defence Investment Plan claims
segments.summary: The live review argues that decade-ahead plan claims should be weighed against what is funded in the next six months to a year.
segments.body: The segment is an organizing hook for current-affairs coverage: long-range statements about future ships can be overtaken by elections and governments, while near-term spending decisions are more concrete.

evidence.note: Transcript contrasts ten-year plan claims with near-term spending and mentions Type 83 timing in relation to Type 26.

segments.title: Pre-war destroyer procurement as a preparedness problem
segments.summary: The video opens with Mahan and preparedness, using the delay in creating war material as the frame for pre-WWI destroyer procurement.
segments.body: This segment grounds the episode in the policy problem behind too-few destroyers: ships cannot be postponed until hostilities are imminent, especially under modern construction timelines.

evidence.note: Opening cites Mahan and preparedness before connecting the argument to destroyer debate and naval construction timing.

segments.title: Bruships 248 question intake reopens during the live stream
segments.summary: The transcript shows the host reopening questions and waiting for the on-screen banner before continuing intake.
segments.body: This first-pass segment records live-stream structure only. Bruships 248 still needs a dedicated Q&A extraction pass to turn audience prompts into useful topical segments.

evidence.note: Transcript says questions are open and references waiting for the banner before proceeding.

segments.title: Yak-38 as the overlooked Cold War V/STOL fighter
segments.summary: The opening positions the Yak-38 as the other Cold War V/STOL fighter, often overshadowed by the Harrier but not simply a failure.
segments.body: The first-pass value is the comparison frame: the transcript notes that the F-35B hover arrangement is closer to Yak-38 engineering than to Harrier, while still calling the Forger a first-generation carrier fighter.

evidence.note: Opening compares Yak-38 with Harrier and F-35B while setting up its first-generation V/STOL role.

segments.title: Press gang myth and reality are separated from the start
segments.summary: The episode opens by contrasting the popular myth of random press-gang seizures with the operational reality of fleet manpower needs.
segments.body: The window gives the page a manpower-policy anchor: press activity is tied to the active fighting season and then narrowed from popular mythology toward how impressment actually operated.

evidence.note: Transcript introduces the topic, active-season fleet growth, the myth, and the first statement of reality.

segments.title: Japanese destroyer fleet is framed around always building
segments.summary: The opening rejects a mysterious Japanese procurement method and instead points to continuous shipbuilding as the practical explanation.
segments.body: This segment improves browsing for modern fleet structure and procurement: the Japan case is presented as a replacement-cycle discipline, not a hidden political trick.

evidence.note: Opening discusses Japanese procurement claims and identifies the method as always building.

segments.title: Bruships 247 opens questions across Discord, Twitch, and YouTube
segments.summary: The transcript marks questions as open across the live platforms, establishing the stream as a Q&A source that needs later extraction.
segments.body: This is a structural segment rather than a substantive naval answer. It keeps the live-stream mechanics visible while preserving the need for a later Q&A pass.

evidence.note: Transcript says questions are open on Discord, Twitch, and YouTube and mentions the bot.

segments.title: HMS Tiger 15-inch upgrade counterfactual starts from the real ship constraints
segments.summary: The episode explicitly splits historical setup from alternate history before moving into HMS Tiger gun and barbette constraints.
segments.body: This first-pass segment marks the format and the technical basis for the counterfactual: the later scenario is meant to rest on real history and ship-design constraints.

evidence.note: Opening explains the history-first structure and introduces the 13.5-inch barbette issue.

segments.title: British Cold War destroyers begin with many unbuilt design studies
segments.summary: The opening frames British Cold War destroyers through the Royal Navy habit of producing many paper and concept studies.
segments.body: The segment anchors later deeper curation around force planning and unbuilt designs: the transcript presents the subject as logical design work for a war that did not happen.

evidence.note: Transcript introduces British Cold War destroyers and emphasizes paper studies and concept designs.

segments.title: Defence Investment Plan live review is conditional on publication
segments.summary: The live stream sets expectations: review the plan if it has appeared, or discuss what should be in it if it has not.
segments.body: This source window is a good first-pass current-affairs anchor because it records the format and contingency before the long live review moves into details.

evidence.note: Opening welcomes viewers to a live review if published or a discussion of what should be included if not.

segments.title: Bruships 246 waits for the questions-open sign
segments.summary: The stream setup tells viewers not to post questions until the questions-open sign is visible.
segments.body: The first-pass segment captures the live intake boundary. The actual naval Q&A content should be extracted in a later pass from the long stream.

evidence.note: Transcript advises waiting for the questions-open sign and then continues stream setup.

segments.title: Fleet organization terminology overview
segments.summary: The episode is introduced as a concise explanation of fleet organization titles and what different groupings mean.
segments.body: The source window makes this a durable reference page for fleet, squadron, flotilla, and related organizational terms rather than a one-off video note.

evidence.note: Opening states the video explains fleet organization and begins defining fleet as a whole navy or constituent part.

segments.title: Oliver Hazard Perry life-extension question sets the live topic
segments.summary: The host frames the central question as whether the US Navy could have modernized Perry-class frigates until a replacement arrived.
segments.body: This is the first usable content window after stream setup and gives the page a clear procurement and modernization anchor for later chaptering.

evidence.note: Transcript states the question about retrofitting and modernizing Oliver Hazard Perry frigates for extended service.

segments.title: Bruships 245 waits for the Discord questions-open signal
segments.summary: The transcript shows the host waiting for the Discord questions-open signal before changing the live banner.
segments.body: This segment is deliberately structural. It marks where the live Q&A system becomes available, while leaving the actual alternate-history and naval-history answers for a later extraction pass.

evidence.note: Transcript references the Discord questions-open signal and banner change.

segments.title: HMS Hermes cruiser-carrier scenario starts from a patron prompt
segments.summary: The video frames a counterfactual around Britain building ten small Hermes-style carriers between 1926 and 1930.
segments.body: The first-pass segment captures why this belongs in both alternate-history and naval-aviation organization: the prompt is treated as a scenario that needs a historically viable path.

evidence.note: Opening reads the small aircraft carrier prompt and states that a viable historical scenario exists.

segments.title: Ram Dass Katari is introduced through modern Indian Navy development
segments.summary: The opening links the Indian destroyer program and indigenization to Katari as a founder figure for the modern Indian Navy.
segments.body: This segment gives the site a leadership path into Indian naval modernization, tying the biography to shipbuilding capacity and destroyer development rather than leaving it as only a person page.

evidence.note: Opening connects Indian destroyers, indigenization, shipbuilding capacity, and Katari as founder of the modern Indian Navy.

segments.title: Combining MEKO and StanFlex raises the problem of too much modularity
segments.summary: The opening asks whether combining a modular frigate design with a modular containerized weapon system would be too much modularity.
segments.body: This first-pass segment creates a stable home for MEKO, StanFlex, and modular ship-design searches. Later chaptering can separate the historical programs from the combined counterfactual.

evidence.note: Opening describes combining modular frigate design with StanFlex and names the resulting thought experiment.

segments.title: Constellation class shifted from conservative design to poor value proposition
segments.summary: The episode asks why a seemingly prudent off-the-shelf frigate became described as 60 percent of a Burke for 80 percent of the cost.
segments.body: This segment gives the page a procurement anchor: the transcript immediately turns from program outcome to US naval procurement incentives, perfection-seeking, risk aversion, and requirements.

evidence.note: Opening states the 60 percent capability for 80 percent cost framing and links it to US naval procurement incentives.

segments.title: Ship design balances history, strategy, and technology
segments.summary: The opening frames warship design as a set of choices shaped by historical memory, strategy, technology, priorities, and criteria.
segments.body: This first-pass segment gives the video a thesis anchor before later chaptering separates examples such as British ASW memory, phased-array radar layouts, carrier geography, and fleet composition.

evidence.note: Opening states that ship design reflects history or memory, strategy, technology, choices, priorities, and criteria.

segments.title: 1920s destroyer crews as the backbone of later capability
segments.summary: The 1920s section treats destroyer crews and experience as the practical backbone for later destroyer capability.
segments.body: This chapter partitions the interwar portion of the episode, where destroyers are not just hull counts but a professional base for later fleet screening.

evidence.note: The 1920s section treats destroyer crews and experience as the practical backbone for later destroyer capability.

segments.title: Destroyer air defence depends on guns and stable platforms
segments.summary: The middle section explains why destroyer air defence needs appropriate guns and stable firing platforms.
segments.body: This separates the anti-aircraft and gunnery argument from the broader screening thesis.

evidence.note: The middle section explains why destroyer air defence needs appropriate guns and stable firing platforms.

segments.title: Modern destroyers become heavy generalist screen ships
segments.summary: The episode identifies the shift toward destroyers as heavy generalists while keeping screening as the background requirement.
segments.body: This point connects Cold War and modern destroyer design to the older escort-screening role.

evidence.note: The episode identifies the shift toward destroyers as heavy generalists while keeping screening as the background requirement.

segments.title: Burke evolution as a long-running destroyer path
segments.summary: The Burke discussion frames the class as an evolved answer to persistent destroyer requirements.
segments.body: This section helps users compare continuity and growth in modern destroyer programs.

evidence.note: The Burke discussion frames the class as an evolved answer to persistent destroyer requirements.

segments.title: Maya and modern fleet-screening endpoints
segments.summary: The later section uses Maya and other modern destroyers to close the argument about screening fleets today.
segments.body: This gives the video a modern endpoint for users browsing Japanese, Burke-like, and high-end screen ships.

evidence.note: The later section uses Maya and other modern destroyers to close the argument about screening fleets today.

segments.title: Durand de la Penne as task-group escort and second ship
segments.summary: The class is described as an important task-group ship, often acting as the second major combatant around a carrier.
segments.body: This chapter distinguishes the ship role from the opening weapons overview.

evidence.note: The class is described as an important task-group ship, often acting as the second major combatant around a carrier.

segments.title: Oto Melara 76 mm development inside Italian destroyer design
segments.summary: The transcript traces the Marina Militare interest in the 76 mm gun and its place in the ship design.
segments.body: This segment supports searches for Oto Melara, gun armament, and Italian destroyer evolution.

evidence.note: The transcript traces the Marina Militare interest in the 76 mm gun and its place in the ship design.

segments.title: Otomat and MILAS create a layered weapons fit
segments.summary: The ship weapons discussion links the 76 mm guns with Otomat and MILAS as part of the class combat system.
segments.body: This point separates missile and ASW strike layers from the gun-focused narrative.

evidence.note: The ship weapons discussion links the 76 mm guns with Otomat and MILAS as part of the class combat system.

segments.title: Garibaldi deployment context for the class
segments.summary: The later section places the class around Giuseppe Garibaldi and allied fleet deployments.
segments.body: This chapter gives the ship a force-package context rather than treating it as an isolated design.

evidence.note: The later section places the class around Giuseppe Garibaldi and allied fleet deployments.

segments.title: Oto Melara legacy and what-if questions for later navies
segments.summary: The conclusion asks how a stronger 76 mm and Otomat legacy might have affected ships such as Type 45.
segments.body: This is a cross-navy design comparison point for users browsing gun and missile tradeoffs.

evidence.note: The conclusion asks how a stronger 76 mm and Otomat legacy might have affected ships such as Type 45.

segments.title: Politics and rules for discussing the Defence Investment Plan
segments.summary: The live review opens by setting expectations for political discussion and orderly questions.
segments.body: This marks the stream framing before the document analysis begins.

evidence.note: The live review opens by setting expectations for political discussion and orderly questions.

segments.title: Defence Investment Plan funding categories are unpacked
segments.summary: The funding section distinguishes actual investment from money already committed or re-labeled.
segments.body: This chapter supports users looking for the budget and procurement critique.

evidence.note: The funding section distinguishes actual investment from money already committed or re-labeled.

segments.title: Recruitment and morale promises get tested against delivery
segments.summary: The review moves from money into recruitment, morale, and whether the plan fixes personnel problems.
segments.body: This separates the manpower critique from the equipment and budget sections.

evidence.note: The review moves from money into recruitment, morale, and whether the plan fixes personnel problems.

segments.title: Quantum and AI language is treated as procurement buzzword risk
segments.summary: The transcript reacts to vague quantum language as buzzword-heavy rather than technically specific.
segments.body: This point is useful for search around technology policy and procurement language.

evidence.note: The transcript reacts to vague quantum language as buzzword-heavy rather than technically specific.

segments.title: Ideal Royal Navy force structure question
segments.summary: An audience question asks what an ideal Royal Navy force would look like.
segments.body: The answer sketches an amphibious/carrier-capable force with LHDs and escort groups, while noting that prior videos cover the idea in more detail.

evidence.note: An audience question asks what an ideal Royal Navy force would look like.

segments.question: What would an ideal Royal Navy look like force-wise?
segments.answerShort: The answer points toward LHDs able to operate F-35Bs plus multiple escort groups, with the caveat that the topic has been covered elsewhere.

segments.title: Air-launched nuclear weapons and NATO DCA question
segments.summary: The stream answers whether air-launched nuclear weapons have a UK use case and why NATO DCA might be purchased into.
segments.body: The response treats aircraft numbers, deterrent options, and the weakness of the proposal as central constraints.

evidence.note: The stream answers whether air-launched nuclear weapons have a UK use case and why NATO DCA might be purchased into.

segments.question: Is there a use case for air-launched nuclear weapons for the RAF, and why buy into NATO DCA if Britain already has a deterrent?
segments.answerShort: There is a possible deterrent use if aircraft numbers and doctrine support it, but the answer presents the plan as a limited and potentially weak extra option.

segments.title: Type 26, Norway, and shipyard claims
segments.summary: The review challenges claims about Norwegian Type 26 work, shipyard fullness, and counting allied ships as British capability.
segments.body: This is a major naval-industrial section of the stream.

evidence.note: The review challenges claims about Norwegian Type 26 work, shipyard fullness, and counting allied ships as British capability.

segments.title: Efficiency savings and reform totals
segments.summary: The late section walks through claimed efficiency savings across workforce, infrastructure, digital, acquisition, and supply chain reforms.
segments.body: This partitions the closing budget-reform material from the earlier document critique.

evidence.note: The late section walks through claimed efficiency savings across workforce, infrastructure, digital, acquisition, and supply chain reforms.

segments.title: Mahan and the destroyer debate
segments.summary: The opening uses Mahan on preparedness to frame why destroyers matter before war begins.
segments.body: This chapter places the destroyer issue inside a broader fleet and preparedness argument.

evidence.note: The opening uses Mahan on preparedness to frame why destroyers matter before war begins.

segments.title: Battleship funding leaves destroyers scarce
segments.summary: The transcript contrasts battleship construction with much smaller destroyer procurement.
segments.body: This segment separates hull-count priorities from the opening policy frame.

evidence.note: The transcript contrasts battleship construction with much smaller destroyer procurement.

segments.title: Wartime destroyer building does not solve preparedness
segments.summary: The episode argues that large World War I destroyer output came too late to count as pre-war preparedness.
segments.body: This point preserves the distinction between wartime mobilization and having forces ready.

evidence.note: The episode argues that large World War I destroyer output came too late to count as pre-war preparedness.

segments.title: Flotilla defence evolves before World War II
segments.summary: The middle section follows how destroyer roles evolve into flotilla defence across the interwar period.
segments.body: This chapter links the pre-WWI scarcity problem to later doctrinal development.

evidence.note: The middle section follows how destroyer roles evolve into flotilla defence across the interwar period.

segments.title: Too few destroyers also have to act as scouts
segments.summary: The late section argues that scarce US destroyers were expected to double as scouts because cruiser numbers were also thin.
segments.body: This shows why destroyer scarcity affected more than torpedo or escort roles.

evidence.note: The late section argues that scarce US destroyers were expected to double as scouts because cruiser numbers were also thin.

segments.title: Useful destroyer force conclusion
segments.summary: The conclusion emphasizes that good destroyers still need useful numbers to provide an effective force.
segments.body: This closes the episode around the gap between ship quality and force quantity.

evidence.note: The conclusion emphasizes that good destroyers still need useful numbers to provide an effective force.

segments.title: 23-knot British battle line question
segments.summary: An audience question asks how Germany would respond if the Orion and King George V battleships matched 23 knots.
segments.body: The answer expects larger and more compromised German responses as the British battle line speed rises.

evidence.note: An audience question asks how Germany would respond if the Orion and King George V battleships matched 23 knots.

segments.question: If the Orion class and following King George V matched 23 knots, how would Germany respond?
segments.answerShort: Likely by building larger ships and making harder design sacrifices to keep up with the faster British battle line.

segments.title: Fire-control data between ships question
segments.summary: A Q&A window explains how ships could share fire-control information when one ship had systems shut down.
segments.body: The answer points to fire-control clocks and displayed data rather than assuming a modern networked system.

evidence.note: A Q&A window explains how ships could share fire-control information when one ship had systems shut down.

segments.question: How can one ship transmit fire-control data to another if that ship has its own system shut out?
segments.answerShort: Use fire-control clock and display arrangements to pass data between ships without relying on the disabled local system.

segments.title: Landlocked country with blue-water warships question
segments.summary: The stream answers whether any landlocked country currently has blue-water warships.
segments.body: The answer is essentially no, with the question treated as a quick force-structure edge case.

evidence.note: The stream answers whether any landlocked country currently has blue-water warships.

segments.question: Is there any landlocked country today with blue-water warships?
segments.answerShort: Probably not; the answer treats the idea as not meaningfully present today.

segments.title: Yorktown, Shokaku, or Illustrious carrier choice
segments.summary: A question asks which carrier class the panel would choose among Yorktown, Shokaku, and Illustrious.
segments.body: The answer depends on theatre, with Illustrious favored in the Mediterranean context.

evidence.note: A question asks which carrier class the panel would choose among Yorktown, Shokaku, and Illustrious.

segments.question: If you had to be on a Yorktown, Shokaku, or Illustrious class carrier, which one would you pick?
segments.answerShort: It depends on theatre; the answer favors Illustrious in the Mediterranean and treats context as decisive.

segments.title: Defence Investment Plan deficiency correction question
segments.summary: A question asks how long it would take to correct Defence Investment Plan deficiencies and rebuild required defence levels.
segments.body: The answer turns on whether the plan survives government changes and whether funding is actually delivered.

evidence.note: A question asks how long it would take to correct Defence Investment Plan deficiencies and rebuild required defence levels.

segments.question: How long would it take to correct deficiencies in the Defence Investment Plan and rebuild defence to required levels?
segments.answerShort: It depends on implementation, future governments, and real funding; the answer rejects a quick fix.

segments.title: Grace Dieu raising question
segments.summary: A viewer asks whether enough of Grace Dieu survives to make raising her worthwhile.
segments.body: The answer is yes from a naval historian perspective, with the caveat that marine archaeology expertise matters.

evidence.note: A viewer asks whether enough of Grace Dieu survives to make raising her worthwhile.

segments.question: Does enough of Grace Dieu survive to make raising her worthwhile?
segments.answerShort: Probably yes from a naval historian perspective, though the answer caveats that marine archaeology expertise is needed.

segments.title: World War I dreadnought threat to a World War II KGV
segments.summary: A question asks how many German World War I dreadnoughts would seriously threaten a World War II King George V.
segments.body: The answer notes that if older heavy guns get close enough, hits can still be a serious event.

evidence.note: A question asks how many German World War I dreadnoughts would seriously threaten a World War II King George V.

segments.question: How many World War I German dreadnoughts would be a significant threat to a World War II KGV?
segments.answerShort: At close enough range, even older heavy guns remain dangerous, so the threat cannot be dismissed purely by age.

segments.summary: The episode stresses learning from other navies, copying what works, and adapting it to Indian needs.
segments.body: This section explains the modernization method behind the leadership profile.

evidence.note: The episode stresses learning from other navies, copying what works, and adapting it to Indian needs.

segments.title: Leadership without enough tools
segments.summary: Katari is presented as showing leadership even when he lacks ideal tools.
segments.body: This point captures the leadership argument in compact form.

evidence.note: Katari is presented as showing leadership even when he lacks ideal tools.

segments.title: Inspections, professionalism, and officer conduct
segments.summary: The later section uses inspection and leadership stories to show Katari professional standards.
segments.body: This chapter grounds the biography in behavior rather than title alone.

evidence.note: The later section uses inspection and leadership stories to show Katari professional standards.

segments.title: Leadership succession and legacy
segments.summary: The close turns to who takes over and why Katari matters to the modern Indian Navy legacy.
segments.body: This final segment gives the page a legacy endpoint.

evidence.note: The close turns to who takes over and why Katari matters to the modern Indian Navy legacy.

segments.title: StanFlex explained inside the Moflex scenario
segments.summary: The episode explains StanFlex modules before combining them with MEKO.
segments.body: This chapter makes the modular weapon-system half of the scenario searchable.

evidence.note: The episode explains StanFlex modules before combining them with MEKO.

segments.title: Harpoon module and weapons payloads
segments.summary: The transcript describes StanFlex missile modules, including Harpoon payload possibilities.
segments.body: This gives the page a missile/weapons segment separate from the general modularity frame.

evidence.note: The transcript describes StanFlex missile modules, including Harpoon payload possibilities.

segments.title: Modularity versus stealth and radar return
segments.summary: The design discussion asks how modular boxes affect radar return and stealth.
segments.body: This point captures the physical integration cost of modularity.

evidence.note: The design discussion asks how modular boxes affect radar return and stealth.

segments.title: Sixteen MEKO nations create a network effect
segments.summary: The scenario expands when the many MEKO operators could adopt StanFlex-style modules.
segments.body: This chapter is the procurement and alliance-scale payoff of Moflex.

evidence.note: The scenario expands when the many MEKO operators could adopt StanFlex-style modules.

segments.title: No major naval war limits visible impact
segments.summary: The conclusion notes that no major naval conflict in the period would have fully tested the changed modular system.
segments.body: This closing caveat prevents the scenario from claiming more historical impact than the transcript supports.

evidence.note: The conclusion notes that no major naval conflict in the period would have fully tested the changed modular system.

segments.title: ASW ship versus multi-mission ship distinction
segments.summary: The video distinguishes FREMM as multi-mission with strong ASW from a purpose-built ASW ship.
segments.body: This chapter anchors the design-comparison section.

evidence.note: The video distinguishes FREMM as multi-mission with strong ASW from a purpose-built ASW ship.

segments.title: VLS cost and capability pressure
segments.summary: The transcript explains why VLS and combat-system expectations can quickly raise frigate costs.
segments.body: This segment captures one route from conservative design to expensive ship.

evidence.note: The transcript explains why VLS and combat-system expectations can quickly raise frigate costs.

segments.title: Burkeization of the frigate requirement
segments.summary: The later design section warns that Burke standards crept into the frigate program.
segments.body: This point is central to the 60-percent-for-80-percent critique.

evidence.note: The later design section warns that Burke standards crept into the frigate program.

segments.title: Bridge layout shows how far the design changed
segments.summary: The comparison of bridge spaces and design stability shows the program moving away from simple FREMM reuse.
segments.body: This section supports the argument that the purchased design was not simply copied.

evidence.note: The comparison of bridge spaces and design stability shows the program moving away from simple FREMM reuse.

segments.title: Functional design instability delayed construction
segments.summary: The GAO-style section says functional design was needed to demonstrate design stability and delayed construction.
segments.body: This is the documentary evidence window for program-management failure.

evidence.note: The GAO-style section says functional design was needed to demonstrate design stability and delayed construction.

segments.title: Copying a bridge does not require buying a design
segments.summary: The conclusion notes that if only a bridge arrangement was wanted, buying the whole design was unnecessary.
segments.body: This point sharpens the critique of what the program actually bought.

evidence.note: The conclusion notes that if only a bridge arrangement was wanted, buying the whole design was unnecessary.

segments.title: Procurement incentives pushed the frigate toward destroyer cost
segments.summary: The opening explains how a prudent off-the-shelf frigate became a poor value proposition.
segments.body: This keeps the headline procurement critique as a granular chapter as well as the overview anchor.

evidence.note: The opening explains how a prudent off-the-shelf frigate became a poor value proposition.

segments.title: Historical memory as shared design knowledge
segments.summary: The early section treats memory as accumulated knowledge that shapes ship design choices.
segments.body: This chapter separates historical memory from later technology and strategy sections.

evidence.note: The early section treats memory as accumulated knowledge that shapes ship design choices.

segments.title: Radar height and phased-array tradeoffs
segments.summary: The technology section explains why radars want height and how multiple radars create design compromises.
segments.body: This gives the page a concrete technology-driven design example.

evidence.note: The technology section explains why radars want height and how multiple radars create design compromises.

segments.title: Carrier design follows geostrategy
segments.summary: The strategy section compares carrier designs and fleet needs across geographies and navies.

evidence.note: The strategy section compares carrier designs and fleet needs across geographies and navies.

segments.title: Battle of the Atlantic as design memory
segments.summary: The video uses the Battle of the Atlantic as an example of memory shaping later naval priorities.
segments.body: This segment connects historical experience to design choices.

evidence.note: The video uses the Battle of the Atlantic as an example of memory shaping later naval priorities.

segments.title: South Atlantic operations affect fleet composition
segments.summary: The fleet-composition section asks what oceans and interests the fleet must serve, including South Atlantic examples.
segments.body: This chapter turns strategy into actual fleet composition.

evidence.note: The fleet-composition section asks what oceans and interests the fleet must serve, including South Atlantic examples.

segments.title: Sending the fleet is an escalation signal
segments.summary: The closing explains that sending a fleet can signal bombardment or coercive escalation, not a friendly message.
segments.body: This is a useful strategic takeaway near the end of the episode.

evidence.note: The closing explains that sending a fleet can signal bombardment or coercive escalation, not a friendly message.
